data.title: Paramount's trinity of CEOs may not survive once the Skydance deal closes - report
data.description:
Paramount Global (NASDAQ:PARA) is expected to undergo sweeping changes, including cuts at the company’s TV networks, allocation of billions more for streaming, and a rejig of the top brass, Bloomberg reported Tuesday, citing people familiar with David Ellison's plans.

Ellison is exploring combining all of Paramount’s TV networks, including CBS and MTV, into one unit, the report said. Those businesses are mostly run by co-CEOs, Chris McCarthy and George Cheeks.

While Cheeks is expected to stay, McCarthy’s future is less certain, the report said. The third co-CEO, Brian Robbins, overseeing Paramount Pictures and Nickelodeon, is expected to leave around the close of the Paramount-Skydance deal, sources told Bloomberg, who asked to not be identified discussing plans that are still being formed.

The report said Ellison and Robbins, who have worked together on several movie titles, are said to have both conceded it’s unlikely Robbins will stick around, but no final decision has been made so far.

Ellison has discussed putting Dana Goldberg, the head of production at Skydance, in charge of the film business, at least for the time being, the report said. He is expected to take charge at the helm of the combined company after the deal closes next year.

data.title: Fed Governor Kugler says she’s taking ‘meeting by meeting’ approach in policy decisions
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Adriana Kugler said Tuesday her decisions about monetary policy will take place “meeting by meeting” as she watches for any changes that may dent the central bank’s progress in curbing inflation.

Kugler, a voter on the Federal Open Market Committee this year, said inflation appears to be on a sustainable path to its 2% target, “even if there have been some bumps along the way,” she said in a speech at an event hosted by the Detroit Economic Club. The labor market remains “solid,” she said.

In assessing incoming data, Kugler expects to see “some bounce back” in the November U.S. nonfarm jobs report due on Friday. The October U.S. nonfarm payrolls released last month showed growth of only +12K jobs, much less than the +125K that was expected.

October’s report “should have shown something much stronger,” but the data took at hit of 100K-120K jobs because of Hurricanes Helene and Milton and a labor strike at Boeing (BA), she said during the Q&A portion of the speech.

Kugler has voted this year to cut the fed funds rate by 75 basis points to the current 4.50%-4.75% range. “I see the Fed's dual-mandate goals of maximum employment and price stability as being roughly in balance,” and policymakers are working to move monetary policy toward a more neutral setting, she said.

“Looking ahead, it is important to emphasize that policy is not on a preset course. I will make decisions meeting by meeting and carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks,” she said. With that, Kugler said she “will vigilantly monitor for incoming risks or negative supply shocks that may undo the progress that we have achieved in reducing inflation.”

The economy has strengthened this year above widely held expectations at the start of 2024, with an increase in immigration and a surge in productivity serving as key drivers, Kugler said.

“We may be getting close” to the economy reaching full employment, she said. “The question is whether we can continue on that,” Kugler said, adding that policymakers will watch "for potential negative supply shocks, potential reversals on immigration, and also maybe changes in trade."

Kugler said it is "too early” to make judgements on immigration policy proposed by the incoming Trump administration. “Studying the specifics, when they come out, will be important, as trade policy may affect productivity and prices,” she said.

Separately, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said Tuesday that he expects interest rates to continue falling in the next year.

data.title: Nebius surges as Citron pounds the table, says CEO is 'the real deal'
data.description:
Nebius (NASDAQ:NBIS) shares surged more than 8% on Tuesday as investment firm Citron Research pounded the table on the artificial intelligence-focused technology firm and said CEO Arkady Volozh is "the real deal."

"Citron pounding the table that $NBIS is poised to be the next AI Wall Street darling," the research firm wrote on the X social network. "A sleeper with no analyst coverage yet, the market hasn’t caught on to its massive potential—or its undervaluation vs. CoreWeave." Shares have gained nearly 35% over the past month.

Nebius, which was once known as Yandex before it severed ties with the Russian firm, was likened to cloud service provider Coreweave by Citron, which is likely to go public next year.

"If it trades at a 50% discount to Coreweave multiple that puts $NBIS at $60 (napkin math)," Citron added, explaining that Nvidia's (NVDA) investment into Nebius was "not out of the blue."

On Monday, Nvidia was among the backers in a $700M private placement financing round by Nebius. Other investors include Accel and certain accounts managed by Orbis Investments. The financing will help Nebius' plans to further build out its full-stack AI infrastructure.

data.title: Chicago Fed's Goolsbee sees rates falling in next year; keeps eye on housing inflation
data.description:
"Over the next year, it feels to me like rates will come down from where we are now," Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said Tuesday during a Q&A. "My belief that rates will and should come down" is based on the data, he added.

The reduction in inflation without sparking a recession was achieved through the healing of supply-side issues.

Monthly payroll employment, a great indicator of the economy, has "broken down a little bit," Goolsbee said. "The inflation series is kind of my pet metric." Specifically, he at housing inflation closely, which is down from its peak, but still higher than it was before the pandemic.

2023 was a "hall of fame year for inflation falling," and marked the only time that happened without triggering a recession. A huge decline in inflation without a recession "is a triumph," he said.

People are still feeling that price levels are higher than they were before the pandemic, he admits. But the Fed's job isn't to lower price levels, but to reduce inflation. The only way to produce deflation — or lowering price levels — is to tank the economy, Goolsbee added.

Breaking news... check back for updates.

data.title: Number of holiday shoppers reaches second-highest on record - National Retail Federation
data.description:
The number of holiday shoppers this year for the five-day period between Thanksgiving and Cyber Monday was down slightly from a year ago but remains the second-highest figure on record and surpassed expectations from the National Retail Federation (“NRF”).

“Thanksgiving weekend retains its prominence among holiday spending events and continues to play a significant role in the holiday season for both consumers and retailers,” NRF President and CEO Matthew Shay said. “Even with this year’s shortened shopping period and the multitude of early sales promotions from retailers, this past weekend exceeded expectations in terms of the sheer volume of shoppers.”

According to data compiled by the NRF, there were 197 million shoppers during the Thanksgiving Day weekend, down 1.7% from last year but 7% more than what was forecasted. And the number of shoppers to brick-and-mortar locations increased from last year by 3.8% to 126 million. For Black Friday alone, 81.7 million shoppers were in stores, up 7% from last year as the rebound from COVID shutdowns continues to reverberate. The most popular destination for shoppers were department stores and online platforms (42%) with 40% of shoppers visiting supermarkets, and 32% heading to discount stores.

Of the 3,0.55 shoppers polled for the survey, they spent an average of $8 more on gifts from last year. The top gifts included clothing and accessories, toys, gift cards, food and candy, and personal care or beauty items. And despite the shorter holiday season, respondents still had more than half of their holiday shopping left to do.

Related Tickers: Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), Mattel (NASDAQ:MAT), Hasbro (NASDAQ:HAS), Best Buy (NYSE:BBY), Walmart (NYSE:WMT), Target (NYSE:TGT), JAKKS Pacific (NASDAQ:JAKK), GameStop (NYSE:GME), Abercrombie & Fitch (NYSE:ANF), Gap (NYSE:GAP), Victoria’s Secret (NYSE:VSCO), Ross Stores (NASDAQ:ROST), TJX Companies (NYSE:TJX), Dillard’s (DDS), Macy’s (NYSE:M), Nordstrom (JWN), Kohl’s (KSS), Foot Locker (NYSE:FL), Deckers Outdoor (NYSE:DECK),eBay (EBAY), Etsy (ETSY), DICK’s Sporting Goods (NYSE:DKS), Bath & Body Works (NYSE:BBWI), Ulta (NASDAQ:ULTA), Five Below (NASDAQ:FIVE), Dollar General (DG), Dollar Tree (DLTR), Burlington Stores (NYSE:BURL), Urban Outfitters (NASDAQ:URBN), SPDR S&P Retail ETF (NYSEARCA:XRT), Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLY).

data.title: Stocks with improving and deteriorating ‘TechniGrade’ by Piper Sandler
data.description:
Piper Sandler analysts put together two lists of stocks with improving and deteriorating “technigrade.”

According to Piper Sandler, this concept compares stocks’ performance within each sector over a period of time.

“With this concept, the performance of each ‘MicroGroup’ is compared to the performance of the MicroGroup universe of nearly 6,000 stocks, known as the ‘MicroGroup Index,’” analysts said. “This process is repeated until a ratio has been calculated over the last six-month period that supplies a heavier weighting to the most recent weeks.”

A TechniGrade rating from 1 (the top 5% of performers) to 20 (bottom 5% of performers) is automatically assigned to each MicroGroup.

According to this TechniGrade concept, financials (XLF) and service sectors are the strongest, and consumer staples (XLP) and health care (XLV) are among the weakest.

Here are the improving TechniGrade rankings:

  • AvidXchange Holdings (AVDX) – Current TechniGrade rank vs. 1 week ago: 3 vs. 4

data.title: Catalent gains amid report buyer working on contact extensions with drugmakers
data.description:
Catalent (NYSE:CTLT) edged higher by 0.8% amid a report that Novo Holdings is working on contract extensions with some drugmakers who are concerned about its $16.5 billion purchase of the contract drug manufacturer.

Novo Holdings is talking to Catalent (CTLT) customers that are worried that they could lose access to Catalent plants once the deal is completed, according to traders, who cited a Capitol Forum report that circulated on Tuesday.

The parties may believe the contract extensions may help the Federal Trade Commission to approve the deal, or it may give the companies a better shot in court if the regulator sues to block the combination. Catalent (CTLT) and Novo Nordisk Foundation received a request for more information from the FTC in May on their planned deal.

The latest update comes after reports last month that the European Union's antitrust regulator will likely grant Catalent (CTLT) approval for its planned sale to Novo Holdings, the parent of Ozempic maker Novo Nordisk (NVO). The European Commission has until Friday to make a decision.

data.title: AngloGold gains as RBC upgrades on cash flow boost from Centamin deal
data.description:
AngloGold Ashanti (NYSE:AU) +5.7% in Tuesday's trading as RBC upgrades shares to Outperform from Sector Perform with a$31 price target, raised from $26, saying the addition of British mining peer Centamin and the 50% interest in the major Sukari mine into its operations will bring a healthy boost to its free cash flow.

The Centamin acquisition is a logical combination that offers net asset value and free cash flow accretion, according to RBC Capital analysts led by Josh Wolfson.

AngloGold's (AU) high free cash flow profile is a key differentiating feature into 2025, Wolfson says, expecting the company to release a new capital return framework to underscore this.

The RBC team forecasts AngloGold's (AU) corporate-level three-year average attributable gold production of 2.9M oz at AISC of ~$1,600/oz and capex of $1.5B; at $2,600/oz gold prices, the company is seen generating annual three-year average attributable EBITDA of $3.8B and free cash flow of $1.5B, highly competitive vs. the peer group universe.

data.title: Intel stuck between foundry and hard place as it searches for new CEO (update)
data.description:
Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) dismissed CEO Pat Gelsinger, who was a driving force behind improving manufacturing capabilities, including the upcoming 18A node, leaving investors and banking firms divided on the best path forward.

"This is going to be a tough job for anyone to step into (it was a tough situation when Pat showed up, and things look much worse now)," said Bernstein Societe Generale Group analysts, led by Stacy Rasgon, in a note. "The choice for any new CEO would seem to center on what to do with the fabs. But while keeping them feels like deadweight, scrapping them would also be fraught with difficulties around the product roadmap, outsourcing strategy, CHIPS act and political navigation."

Possible replacements have already been mentioned. Lip-Bu Tan, a former Intel board of directors member and CEO of Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) from 2009 to 2021, is on the shortlist of potential candidates, according to Reuters.

Marvell Technology (MRVL) Chief Executive Officer Mark Murphy has also been mentioned, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter. The company is working with executive search firm Spencer Stuart to find a replacement for Gelsinger, the news outlet added.

Marvell has not yet responded to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Citi Research believes scrapping the foundry dreams remains the best option, at least in terms of the shareholders.

"We believe Intel has a slim chance of succeeding in the merchant foundry business, and the company should exit the merchant foundry business," said Citi analyst Christopher Danely, in a note. "We believe if Intel exits foundry, gross margins could rise to the low to mid 50% range and EPS in the $3.00-$4.00 range, which could translate to a $50-$60 stock."

BofA Securities highlights how the $7.86B CHIPS Act award prevents Intel from fully separating from its foundry business, even if that is in the best interest of the company. Intel must maintain at least 50.1% ownership of Intel Foundry and cannot cede 35% or more of Intel Foundry ownership to a third party if it is listed as a public company.

"INTC continues to cede PC/server shares to Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Arm Holdings (ARM)," said BofA analyst Vivek Arya, in a note. "Meanwhile, PC demand outlook remains grim, with Dell (DELL)/HP (HPQ) suggesting a delayed PC refresh cycle into CY25. We also highlight 2H25 costs could get worse for INTC given early start-up costs for its 18A node, which is still to get any external validation from a large fabless customer."

Intel 18A is a new process node from Intel Foundry designed for high-performance computing and mobile applications. It is expected to help Intel compete with semiconductor stalwarts such as TSMC (TSM) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). Intel plans to launch it in 2025.

Intel shares were down more than 5% on Tuesday.

(This story has been updated to include Marvell CEO Mark Murphy has also reportedly been contacted.)
